page: Vultr vx1-m-48c-384gb is a memory-optimized server instance from the VX1 Cloud Compute family. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an AMD EPYC processor with 48 vCPUs and 24 physical cores operating at 2.0 GHz. The system is equipped with 384.0 GB of RAM, providing 8.0 GB of memory per core. It does not include a GPU and comes with a minimal 1 GB of network-attached local storage, requiring external storage solutions for data-heavy applications. A single complimentary public IPv4 address is included.

Benchmark data indicates strong performance across multiple vectors. Single-core CPU performance via stress-ng is in the top tier (top 10%), while multi-core performance sits in the strong top 25% bracket. Large-block memory bandwidth reads and writes are highly efficient, both scoring in the top 10%. Single-threaded gzip compression and bzip3 decompression also achieve top-tier status. However, Passmark memory scores and medium-to-large LLM prompt processing speeds fall into the average category.

The primary tradeoff of the vx1-m-48c-384gb is its shared CPU allocation, which may introduce resource contention compared to dedicated instances. Additionally, the lack of substantial local storage means users must provision network storage separately. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the shared CPU model combined with a high memory-to-core ratio provides a cost-effective option for memory-intensive workloads that do not require continuous, dedicated CPU performance.

This instance is well-suited for memory-intensive applications such as large-scale caching, in-memory databases like Redis, static web serving, and moderate-scale data processing. It is also capable of handling light LLM inference tasks and general-purpose workloads requiring high memory capacity without the premium cost of dedicated CPU cores.
description: Vultr vx1-m-48c-384gb is an x86_64 memory-optimized server within the VX1 Cloud Compute family, featuring 48 vCPUs and 24 physical AMD EPYC cores running at 2.0 GHz. It is configured with 384.0 GB of RAM, yielding 8.0 GB per core, but lacks a GPU and provides only 1 GB of network storage. Benchmarks show top-tier single-core CPU performance and large-block memory bandwidth, alongside strong multi-core and database execution speeds. However, its shared CPU allocation presents a tradeoff in processing consistency, and Passmark memory scores remain average. Qualitatively, this configuration offers high memory density at a lower cost point due to the shared CPU model. It is best suited for in-memory databases, caching layers, static web serving, and memory-heavy workloads that do not require dedicated CPU resources.

tagline: A high-density, memory-optimized shared instance featuring forty-eight vCPUs and three hundred eighty-four gigabytes of RAM for memory-intensive workloads.
